title: Clean Fuels Weekly Commentary: LCFS Spot Prices Decline While Transfer Volumes Rise Nearly 240% Week Over Week
content:
This Weekâ€™s Highlights

(20th â€“ 26th October 2025)
  • A total of 351,318 credits were exchanged as Type 1 trades, a rise of 13.56% week-over-week (WoW), and all non-zero trades witnessed a significant increase of 240.72% WoW as 2,875,107 credits were traded in CA LCFS.
  • The weekly average CA LCFS credit price stood at $54.66/MT for Type 1 transfers, recording a 4.36% fall WoW; All Non-Zero transfers saw a rise of 4.66%, priced at $59.28/MT.
  • The average LCFS spot credit price declined by 1.07% WoW; on 29th October, the spot price stood at $54.75/MT, and the benchmark price stood at $55.13/MT.
  • D3 RIN prices rose by 2.55% WoW. Both D4 and D5 fell by 2.97% WoW, and D6 prices fell by 0.98% WoW.

cCarbonâ€™s Market View

Last week, the California Air Resources Board (CARB) held a public workshop to discuss potential updates to the stateâ€™s next phase of light-duty vehicle emissions standards for the 2030s and beyond. The agency has officially initiated its rulemaking process, announcing that it plans to release an initial staff report in fall or winter 2026, ahead of presenting a formal proposal to board members. At the federal level, rent fuel market conditions. In a letter to lawmakers, API President and CEO Mike Sommers claimed that the significant shifts in federal biofuel policy, state volatility mandates, and market dynamics have transformed the fuels landscape.

CA LCFS Weekly Price and Volume Trend

[caption id="attachment_606691" align="alignnone" width="696"] Table 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average Type 1 transfer prices recorded a 4.36% WoW decrease, falling from $57.15 to $54.66 this week, and all non-zero transfer prices rose from $56.64 to $59.28, recording a rise of 4.66%. Type 1 trades witnessed 351,381 credits exchanged, recording a 13.56% rise WoW. The total transaction volumes for all non-zero trades saw an increase of 240.72% WoW, reaching 2,875,107 credits. [caption id="attachment_606692" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption]

CA LCFS Daily Average Spot Price

[caption id="attachment_606693" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 2. California LCFS Weekly average spot prices and benchmark (*CC.Info OTC: Numbers are an average of broker prices reported to CC.info)[/caption] Last week, the average LCFS spot credit prices fell by 1.07% WoW, reaching $54.56/MT. On 29th October, the spot price stood at $54.75/MT. The benchmark price, representing the future price of contracts expiring at the end of 2025, stood at $55.13/MT on 29th October.

LCFS Weekly Trade Frequency and Volume

[caption id="attachment_606694" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 3. Trade frequency and volume, Source: California ARB[/caption] As per the data reported by CARB last week, 102 trades were reported in the market, with 13 high-volume trades. The highest transfer by price was $82.00 for a Type 1 transaction at a volume of 5,000 credits. The lowest transfer by price was $

CA LCFS Monthly Volumes and Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_606695" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 4. Total monthly volumes and associated prices, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average spot Type 1 price stood at $53.76 in October so far, falling from $53.94 in September. Trade volumes for the month of October stood at 12.02 M as of 29th October, 2025. This month has witnessed the highest monthly transaction volumes ever in the history of the program.

CA LCFS Weekly Volume Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_606696" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 5. Volume Price Trend indicator for the CA LCFS market, Source: California ARB[/caption] The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for a stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls. Across 20th â€“ 26th October 2025, total VPT rose by 12.89% WoW, with a rise in all non-zero credit prices for the last week.

Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS): RIN Weekly Price Trend

Â RINs are credits used for compliance and are the â€œcurrencyâ€ of the RFS program. Renewable fuel producers generate RINs, market participants trade RINs, and obligated parties obtain and then ultimately retire RINs for the compliance list. D3 RIN prices rose by 2.55% WoW. Both D4 and D5 fell by 2.97% WoW, and D6 prices fell by 0.98% WoW. D3: Cellulosic biofuel D4: Biomass-based diesel D5: Advanced biofuels (produced from any type of renewable biomass except corn starch ethanol) D6: Conventional renewable biofuel ethanol derived from corn starch, or any other qualifying renewable fuel

Upcoming DatesÂ 

Key Clean Fuels Market Developments This Week

  • API Calls for Balanced E15 Policy Amid Shifting Market Realities: The American Petroleum Institute (API) has urged congressional leadership to adopt a more balanced and up-to-date approach to E15 legislation, warning the current proposal no longer reflects today's fuel market realities. In a letter to Senate Majority Leader John Thune, Senate Minority Leader Charles Schumer, Speaker of the House Mike Johnson and House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries, API President and CEO Mike Sommers said sweeping changes to federal biofuel policy, state volatility mandates, and market conditions have upended the fuels landscape, prompting API to oppose the Nationwide Consumer and Fuel Retailer Choice Act of 2025 in its current form.
  • CHARBONE Announces a Strategic Alliance with a Leading U.S. Industrial Gas Producer: Charbone Corporation, a North American producer and distributor specializing in clean Ultra High Purity (â€œUHPâ€) hydrogen and strategic industrial gases, is pleased to announce the conclusion of a strategic alliance and multiple commercial supply agreements with a major U.S. producer and distributor of industrial gases headquartered in USA, a subsidiary of a member of one of the worldâ€™s largest chemical and industrial conglomerates. This alliance will enable CHARBONE to expand its product offering by adding high-value gasesâ€”most notably heliumâ€”while strengthening its positioning in the North American markets for low-carbon and ultra-high-purity gases. Through this collaboration, CHARBONE enhances its operational flexibility, optimizes the use of its logistics and transport assets, and establishes strategic synergies with a globally recognized partner. This partnership represents a major milestone in CHARBONEâ€™s long-term growth strategy, which aims to diversify its revenue streams and consolidate its footprint in the Canadian market.
  • Public Workshop by CARB on Drive Forward Light-Duty Vehicle Program: The California Air Resources Board hosted a public workshop to inform potential updates to Californiaâ€™s next round of light-duty vehicle emissions standards for the 2030s and beyond. The CARB has officially launched its rulemaking on new standards for light-duty vehicles, telling participants during a workshop that the agency plans to release its initial staff report in fall or winter 2026 before bringing a proposal to board members.
  • Ecologyâ€™s $25 Million â€œRails, Keels, and Wheelsâ€ Grant to Fund Zero-Emission Vehicles is Now Open: Tugboats, street sweepers, freight switchers, ambulances, and box trucks typically run on diesel engines that produce harmful exhaust. But there is cleaner technology available, and a new, $25Â million grant program from the Washington Department of Ecology aims to put more of those clean vehicles to work. Ecology Director Casey Sixkiller says this is a unique moment for both businesses and nonprofits to shape diesel reduction efforts in Washington.
